steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures around the blank
steps.content:
The left side is an independent clause: “Such interpretations are ultimately speculative” (subject + verb + complement).

The right side is a complete clause that will become dependent once “though” is placed before it: “the object's gray coloration may be an incidental effect...”

This is a joining problem: we need the right punctuation to connect an independent clause to a dependent clause.

steps.title: Apply the subordinating conjunction rule
steps.content:
”Though” is a subordinating conjunction — it introduces a dependent (subordinate) clause. The rule: join an independent clause to a dependent clause with a comma.

The correct form is IC, SC: “Such interpretations are ultimately speculative, though the object's gray coloration may be an incidental effect...”

Choice A introduces a semicolon after “though” (breaking the dependent clause with extra punctuation), choice B adds a comma after “though” (creating a comma splice), and choice C uses a semicolon before the dependent clause (violating the subordinating conjunction rule—semicolons join two independent clauses, not IC + DC).

That matches choice D — D is correct.
